source_paper_title: Risk Sharing with Lambda Value at Risk
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Liu_2025, title={Risk Sharing with Lambda Value at Risk}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0246}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0246}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Liu, Peng}, year={2025}, month=feb, pages={313–333} }
context:
Section 1 Introduction (page 3) and Section 6 Conclusion (page 17).

The inf-convolution of two \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) is a very difficult problem and is beyond the scope of this paper as it involves the robust risk aggregation of two \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) with fixed marginal distributions and unknown dependence structure.

There are still some unsolved problems such as the inf-convolution of multiple \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\), and the inf-convolution of multiple \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}\) under heterogenous beliefs.
title: Characterize inf-convolution of multiple Lambda value-at-risk plus risk measures setting
background_latex:
Let \((\Omega,\mathcal F,\mathbb P)\) be an atomless probability space and let \(\mathcal X\) be a linear space of real-valued random variables (losses) with \(L^\infty\subseteq \mathcal X\subseteq L^1\). For \(X\in\mathcal X\), write \(F_X\) for its distribution function and \(F_X^{-1}(p)=\inf\{x\in\mathbb R: F_X(x)\ge p\}\) for its left-quantile, \(p\in(0,1]\). An \(n\)-allocation of an aggregate loss \(X\) is an \(n\)-tuple \((X_1,\dots,X_n)\in\mathcal X^n\) with \(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i=X\) almost surely.

A (not necessarily cash-additive) risk measure is a map \(\rho:\mathcal X\to(-\infty,\infty]\). Given risk measures \(\rho_1,\dots,\rho_n\), their inf-convolution is
\[\big(\square_{i=1}^n \rho_i\big)(X)=\inf\Big\{\sum_{i=1}^n \rho_i(X_i): (X_1,\dots,X_n)\in\mathcal X^n,\ \sum_{i=1}^n X_i=X\Big\}.\]

Fix a right-continuous function \(\Lambda:\mathbb R\to[0,1]\) that is not identically equal to \(1\). Define the Lambda value-at-risk plus functional by
\[\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+(X)=\sup\{x\in\mathbb R: F_X(x)< 1-\Lambda(x)\},\]
with the conventions \(\sup\emptyset=-\infty\) and \(\inf\emptyset=\infty\) (when needed). For multiple agents, allow \(\Lambda_i\) and corresponding \(\Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\), \(i=1,\dots,n\).

The paper studies risk sharing via inf-convolution for \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}\) and for \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) combined with another risk measure under additional structure, but explicitly states that the case of inf-convolving multiple \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) remains open.
problem_definition_latex:
Given \(n\ge 2\) right-continuous functions \(\Lambda_i:\mathbb R\to[0,1]\) (not identically \(1\)) and the associated risk measures \(\rho_i=\Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\) on \(\mathcal X\), determine (i) an explicit or tractable characterization of the inf-convolution
\[\big(\square_{i=1}^n \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\big)(X)=\inf\Big\{\sum_{i=1}^n \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+(X_i): (X_1,\dots,X_n)\in\mathcal X^n,\ \sum_{i=1}^n X_i=X\Big\}\]
and (ii) the structure (and existence conditions) of optimal allocations \((X_1,\dots,X_n)\) attaining the infimum, as functions of \(X\) and \((\Lambda_1,\dots,\Lambda_n)\).
latex:
Let \((\Omega,\mathcal F,\mathbb P)\) be an atomless probability space and let \(\mathcal X\) be a linear space of real-valued random variables (losses) with \(L^\infty\subseteq \mathcal X\subseteq L^1\). For \(X\in\mathcal X\), write \(F_X\) for its distribution function and \(F_X^{-1}(p)=\inf\{x\in\mathbb R: F_X(x)\ge p\}\) for its left-quantile, \(p\in(0,1]\). An \(n\)-allocation of an aggregate loss \(X\) is an \(n\)-tuple \((X_1,\dots,X_n)\in\mathcal X^n\) with \(\sum_{i=1}^n X_i=X\) almost surely.

A (not necessarily cash-additive) risk measure is a map \(\rho:\mathcal X\to(-\infty,\infty]\). Given risk measures \(\rho_1,\dots,\rho_n\), their inf-convolution is
\[\big(\square_{i=1}^n \rho_i\big)(X)=\inf\Big\{\sum_{i=1}^n \rho_i(X_i): (X_1,\dots,X_n)\in\mathcal X^n,\ \sum_{i=1}^n X_i=X\Big\}.\]

Fix a right-continuous function \(\Lambda:\mathbb R\to[0,1]\) that is not identically equal to \(1\). Define the Lambda value-at-risk plus functional by
\[\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+(X)=\sup\{x\in\mathbb R: F_X(x)< 1-\Lambda(x)\},\]
with the conventions \(\sup\emptyset=-\infty\) and \(\inf\emptyset=\infty\) (when needed). For multiple agents, allow \(\Lambda_i\) and corresponding \(\Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\), \(i=1,\dots,n\).

The paper studies risk sharing via inf-convolution for \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}\) and for \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) combined with another risk measure under additional structure, but explicitly states that the case of inf-convolving multiple \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) remains open.

Given \(n\ge 2\) right-continuous functions \(\Lambda_i:\mathbb R\to[0,1]\) (not identically \(1\)) and the associated risk measures \(\rho_i=\Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\) on \(\mathcal X\), determine (i) an explicit or tractable characterization of the inf-convolution
\[\big(\square_{i=1}^n \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\big)(X)=\inf\Big\{\sum_{i=1}^n \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+(X_i): (X_1,\dots,X_n)\in\mathcal X^n,\ \sum_{i=1}^n X_i=X\Big\}\]
and (ii) the structure (and existence conditions) of optimal allocations \((X_1,\dots,X_n)\) attaining the infimum, as functions of \(X\) and \((\Lambda_1,\dots,\Lambda_n)\).
open_problem_quote:
The inf-convolution of two \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) is a very difficult problem and is beyond the scope of this paper as it involves the robust risk aggregation of two \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) with fixed marginal distributions and unknown dependence structure.

There are still some unsolved problems such as the inf-convolution of multiple \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\), and the inf-convolution of multiple \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}\) under heterogenous beliefs.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can numerically search over finite-dimensional parametric families of \(\Lambda_i\), discrete loss distributions, and dependence couplings to probe candidate formulas or conjectured allocation structures, but the universal quantification over all \(X\) and couplings makes definitive counterexamples less straightforward.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate constructions (e.g., allocation rules, bounds, or conjectured closed forms for restricted classes) can be represented programmatically and evaluated by computing \(\sum_i \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+(X_i)\) under constraints \(\sum_i X_i=X\) on discretized spaces; however, the inner optimization and dependence-robust aspects can make the fitness signal expensive and only approximate the full general problem.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Liu (2023), the inf-convolution problem for multiple agents is solved in considerable generality for \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}\) (the inf-quantile definition): when the \(\Lambda_i\) are monotone in the same direction and mild boundary/attainability conditions hold, \(\square_{i=1}^n \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}\) admits an explicit characterization as \(\inf_{x_{n-1}} \Lambda^{x_{n-1}}\mathrm{VaR}(X)\), and in favorable cases collapses to a single \(\Lambda^*\mathrm{VaR}(X)\) where \(\Lambda^*(x)=\sup_{\sum y_i=x}(\sum_i \Lambda_i(y_i))\wedge 1\). Optimal allocations can be constructed by partitioning the probability space according to the uniform transform \(U_X\) and assigning tail events to agents, closely paralleling classical VaR risk sharing.

For the plus-variant \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\), Liu (2023) shows that even the two-agent inf-convolution is substantially harder because \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) interacts with dependence/robust aggregation effects; the paper therefore treats \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\square \rho\) only when \(\rho\) is SSD-consistent, yielding an optimization over \((x,y)\) and an optimal allocation of the form \(X_1^*=F^{-1}_{x^*,y^*}(U_X)\), \(X_2^*=X-X_1^*\), which is generally neither comonotone nor a simple tail-event split. Subsequent work on Lambda-VaR under ambiguity and optimal risk sharing for lambda value-at-risk provides strong partial progress in the multi-agent direction, but for \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}\) (and capacity/robust extensions) rather than \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\): these papers establish closure under inf-convolution and give explicit event/capacity characterizations and tail-splitting optimal allocations when \(\Lambda_i\) are monotone.

At present, there is no paper in the provided forward-citation set that gives a tractable explicit characterization of \(\square_{i=1}^n \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\) for general \(n\ge 3\) under a probability measure, nor a general structural theorem for existence and form of optimal allocations. Related advances on nonconvex quantile-based risk sharing (e.g., averaged-quantile inf-convolutions) and on robust \(\Lambda\)-quantiles via extremal distributions suggest that progress will likely require combining (i) tail-splitting/partition constructions with (ii) robust aggregation or extremal-coupling arguments to handle the dependence uncertainty intrinsic to \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\).

sources.title: Optimal risk sharing for lambda value-at-risk
sources.theorem_or_result:
For monotone (in the same direction) Lambda functions, the paper proves closure of the class \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^w\) under inf-convolution and gives an explicit downset/capacity characterization and optimal allocations. Concretely, if \(\rho_i(X)=\Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^{w_i}(X)=\inf\{x\in\mathbb R: w_i(X>x)\le \Lambda_i(x)\}\) with continuous capacities \(w_i\) and \(\Lambda_i\in H_I\) (all increasing) or \(\Lambda_i\in H_D\) (all decreasing), then
- \(\square_{i=1}^n \rho_i(X)=\inf\{x\in\mathbb R: \{X>x\}\in A^{\Lambda,w}_x\}\), where \(A^{\Lambda,w}_x=\{\cup_{i=1}^n A_i: w_i(A_i)\le \Lambda_i(y_i),\ \sum_i y_i=x\}\) (Proposition 8 / Theorem 3).
- Moreover \(\square_{i=1}^n \rho_i\) is itself of the form \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^w\) for some monotone \(\Lambda\) and capacity \(w\) (Proposition 8).
- If the infimum is attained at \(x^*\), an optimal allocation is \(X_i=(X-x^*)\mathbf 1_{A_i^*}+y_i^*\) with \(\sum_i y_i^*=x^*\) and \(w_i(\{X>x^*\}\cap A_i^*)\le \Lambda_i(y_i^*)\) (Theorem 3 / Proposition 8).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for a tractable characterization of \(\square_{i=1}^n \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\) (multiple agents) and the structure/existence of optimal allocations. This paper does not directly treat \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) under a probability (it focuses on \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}\) and \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) under capacities, but the risk-sharing/inf-convolution results in Section 5 are for \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^w\), not \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^{+,w}\)). However, it makes strong partial progress by:
- Introducing an equivalent representation of \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^w\) (for monotone \(\Lambda\)) via families of downsets (Theorem 1), and proving that induced risk measures are closed under inf-convolution (Theorem 3).
- Applying this to obtain an explicit event/capacity characterization of \(\square_i \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^{w_i}\) and explicit optimal allocations (Proposition 8).
Thus it essentially resolves the analogous ‘multiple \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}\)’ inf-convolution problem (and under ambiguity/capacities), and provides tools likely adaptable to \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\). But it does not claim or show a full characterization for \(\square_i \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\) as posed in the original paper, so it is not a complete solution to that open problem.

sources.title: Lambda Value-at-Risk under ambiguity and risk sharing
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves a general inf-convolution/optimal-allocation theorem for Lambda-VaR under capacities and monotone Lambda. In particular, for continuous capacities \(w_i\) and \(\Lambda_i\in H_I\) (or all \(\Lambda_i\in H_D\)), it shows
\[
\Big(\square_{i=1}^n \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^{w_i}\Big)(X)=\inf\{x\in\u211d:{\{X>x\}}\in A^{\Lambda,w}_x\},
\]
where \(A^{\Lambda,w}_x=\{\cup_{i=1}^n A_i: \exists y_1+\cdots+y_n=x\text{ with }w_i(A_i)\le \Lambda_i(y_i)\}\). Moreover, if a minimizer \(x^*\) exists, an optimal allocation is
\[
X_i=(X-x^*)\mathbf 1_{A_i^*}+y_i^*,\quad \sum_i y_i^*=x^*,\quad w_i(\{X>x^*\}\cap A_i^*)\le \Lambda_i(y_i^*).
\]
It further shows closure: if all \(\Lambda_i\) are monotone in the same direction, then \(\square_i \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^{w_i}\) is again of the form \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^w\) for some capacity \(w\) and some \(\Lambda\in H_I^*\) (resp. \(H_D^*\)).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an explicit/tractable characterization of \(\square_i \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\) (the plus version under a probability) and the structure/existence of optimal allocations. This paper does not directly treat \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) inf-convolution. Instead it develops a parallel theory for \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^w\) (the inf version) under capacities and proves a general inf-convolution formula and explicit optimal allocations via families of downsets (Theorem 3, Proposition 8). These results are highly relevant because \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) is the dual/sup-quantile analogue (they define \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^{+,w}\) too), and the open problem concerns the difficulty of handling multiple \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\). The papers downset/partition method and closure-under-inf-convolution results provide strong tools and suggest a route to attack the \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) case, but it does not provide the requested characterization for \(\square_i \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\) itself.

sources.title: Risk sharing with Lambda value at risk under heterogeneous beliefs
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper derives semi-explicit characterizations and optimal allocations for inf-convolutions involving Lambda Value-at-Risk under heterogeneous beliefs, and for VaR+ only in the two-agent (or one VaR+ plus a general monotone risk measure) setting. In particular, Theorem 6 shows for \mathrm{VaR}^{+ ,Q_1} and a monotone law-invariant \(\rho^{Q_2}\):
\[\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^{+,Q_1}\square \rho^{Q_2}(X)=\inf_{x\in\mathbb R}\inf_{y\ge x}\inf_{U\sim U[0,1]\text{ under }Q_1}\Big\{x+\rho^{Q_2}\big(X-\Lambda^{-1}_{x,y}(U)\big)\Big\},\]
with an optimal allocation \(X_1^*=\Lambda^{-1}_{x^*,y^*}(U^*),\ X_2^*=X-X_1^*\) when a minimizer exists. Taking \(\rho=\Lambda_2\mathrm{VaR}^{+,Q_2}\) yields an (implicit) formula for the inf-convolution of two \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) and the corresponding optimal allocation.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for an explicit/tractable characterization of \(\square_{i=1}^n \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\) and optimal allocations for general \(n\ge2\) on an atomless space. This paper does not provide a full characterization for multiple (\(n\ge3\)) \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) inf-convolution; indeed it states this remains unsolved. However, it makes substantive progress by:
- Providing a general variational representation and optimal allocation structure for \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\square \rho\) (Theorem 6), which includes the special case of two \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) (set \(\rho=\Lambda_2\mathrm{VaR}^+\)).
- Showing the optimal allocation for the two-agent \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) case has a non-comonotone/non-pairwise-countermonotone form \(X_1^*=\Lambda^{-1}_{x^*,y^*}(U^*)\), highlighting structural differences from the \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}\) case.
Thus it partially addresses (i)-(ii) only for \(n=2\) (and more generally \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) with another risk measure), not for general \(n\).

sources.title: Extended Convolution Bounds on the Fr\'{e} chet Problem: Robust Risk Aggregation and Risk Sharing
sources.theorem_or_result:
Theorem 5 (Risk sharing for averaged quantiles). For X\in L^1\) and parameters \(\beta_i\in(0,1)\) with \(\beta:=\sum_{i=1}^n\beta_i\in(0,1)\), let \(I_i=[0,\beta_i]\cup[1-\beta+\beta_i,1]\). Then the inf-convolution of the average-quantile risk measures \(R_{I_i}\) satisfies
\[
\square_{i=1}^n R_{I_i}(X)=R_{[0,\beta]}(X).
\]
If \(X\) is bounded above, an optimal allocation exists and can be chosen as
\(X_i=(X-t)\mathbf 1_{A_i}+\frac{X}{n}\mathbf 1_{A^c}+\frac{t}{n-1}\mathbf 1_{A\setminus A_i}\), where \(A=\{U_X\le \beta\}\), \((A_i)\) partitions \(A\) with \(\mathbb P(A_i)=\beta_i\), and \(t\ge (q^-_1(X))^+\). If \(X\) is unbounded above, no optimal allocation attains the infimum (Theorem 6), but a sequence of allocations approaches it.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an explicit characterization of \(\square_{i=1}^n \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\) and optimal allocations. This paper does not study \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) or its inf-convolution directly. However, it solves a closely related nonconvex risk-sharing/inf-convolution problem for a class of quantile-based (average-quantile) risk measures \(R_I\) where \(I\) is a union of two tail intervals \([0,\beta_i]\cup[1-\beta+\beta_i,1]\). The resulting inf-convolution collapses to a single lower-tail averaged quantile \(R_{[0,\beta]}(X)\), and the paper gives explicit optimal allocations and an existence criterion (boundedness above). Since \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\) is also defined via distribution/quantile-type constraints, the techniques (new RVaR inequality, tail-splitting allocations, dependence-structure arguments for non-attainment) may be adaptable tools toward characterizing inf-convolutions of multiple \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\), but no direct formula for \(\square_i \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\) is provided here.

sources.title: Robust Lambda-quantiles and extreme probabilities
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper’s main result (Theorem 1) shows that robust (worst-/best-case) \(\Lambda\)-quantiles over an uncertainty set \(\mathcal M\) can be computed as \(\Lambda\)-quantiles of the extremal distribution functions \(F^-_{\mathcal M}(x)=\inf_{F\in\mathcal M}F(x)\) and \(F^+_{\mathcal M}(x)=\sup_{F\in\mathcal M}F(x)\), with additional attainability conditions for some of the four \(\Lambda\)-quantile variants. In particular, for the Bellini–Peri variants \(\tilde q^-_\Lambda, q^+_\Lambda\) one always has \(\sup_{F\in\mathcal M}\tilde q^-_\Lambda(F)=\tilde q^-_\Lambda(F^-_{\mathcal M})\) and \(\inf_{F\in\mathcal M} q^+_\Lambda(F)= q^+_\Lambda(F^+_{\mathcal M})\); for the other variants equalities hold under attainability of \(F^-_{\mathcal M}\) or \(F^+_{\mathcal M}\), and simplify when \(\Lambda\) is decreasing.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an explicit characterization of the inf-convolution \(\square_{i=1}^n \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\) and optimal allocations. This paper does not study inf-convolution or risk sharing/allocations. It studies distributional robustness of \(\Lambda\)-quantiles (including variants closely related to \(\Lambda\mathrm{VaR}^+\)) and provides extremal-distribution formulas for worst-/best-case \(\Lambda\)-quantiles under moment/Wasserstein/marginal-aggregation uncertainty sets. These extremal-distribution techniques might be tangentially useful if one reformulates inf-convolution problems via distributional bounds or aggregation sets, but no direct results on \(\square_i \Lambda_i\mathrm{VaR}^+\) are given.
